items.q: Why might managed retreat be more sustainable than building a sea wall?
items.correct: Sea walls are expensive, deflect erosion elsewhere and reflect wave energy worsening beach loss; managed retreat works with natural processes, creates habitat and is cheaper long-term.
items.misconception: Sea walls always solve coastal erosion problems permanently.
items.random_wrong: Managed retreat means letting all coastal towns disappear into the sea.
items.why: Sea walls protect one stretch but cause terminal scour and sediment starvation downdrift. Managed retreat selectively allows the coast to adjust — controversial socially but ecologically sound.

items.q: What is the difference between hard and soft engineering for flood management?
items.correct: Hard engineering uses built structures (dams, levees, channelisation) to control flow; soft engineering works with natural processes (afforestation, wetland restoration, floodplain zoning).
items.misconception: Hard engineering always works better than soft engineering at preventing floods.
items.random_wrong: Soft engineering means using softer materials like sand instead of concrete.
items.why: Hard engineering is expensive, ecologically damaging and can shift problems downstream. Soft approaches are cheaper and sustainable but slower to act — a typical evaluation question.

items.q: What is the enhanced greenhouse effect?
items.correct: The strengthening of the natural greenhouse effect by human activities (mainly burning fossil fuels) which add CO2 and other gases to the atmosphere.
items.misconception: The greenhouse effect is entirely man-made and harmful.
items.random_wrong: It is the hole in the ozone layer letting in more sun.
items.why: The natural greenhouse effect is essential for life — without it Earth would be -18 degrees C. The problem is enhancement. Students also confuse it with the (separate) ozone hole.

items.q: What is the average annual retreat rate at Holderness?
items.why: Correct answer: 1.8 m/yr. “0.3 m/yr” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which is an example of soft engineering?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Beach nourishment. “Sea wall” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many villages have been lost on the Holderness coast since Roman times?
items.why: Exact answer: 29. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
